name: Himeji Castle
shortDescription: Japan's most spectacular castle, the 'White Heron' — never destroyed or rebuilt.
description: Himeji is Japan's finest surviving castle and a masterpiece of Japanese defensive architecture. Its brilliant white plaster walls earned it the nickname 'White Heron Castle.' The castle complex consists of 83 buildings with an elaborate system of defensive zones using winding pathways, dead ends, and loopholes to funnel attackers into killing grounds. Uniquely, it has never been destroyed in battle or fire and survives in its original form.

name: Matsumoto Castle
shortDescription: The 'Crow Castle' — one of Japan's oldest surviving original tenshu (main tower).
description: Matsumoto's striking black-and-white main tower is one of Japan's twelve surviving original castle keeps. Known as Karasu-jō ('Crow Castle') for its dark exterior, it was built in the late Sengoku period and features a rare 'moon-viewing turret' added during the peaceful Edo period. The castle is reflected in its surrounding moat, offering one of the most iconic images in Japanese architecture.

name: Osaka Castle
shortDescription: Toyotomi Hideyoshi's great castle — once the largest in Japan.
description: Built by Toyotomi Hideyoshi in 1583 after he reunified Japan, Osaka Castle was intended to be the greatest fortress in the land. Its massive stone walls, wide moats, and five-story main tower dominated the landscape. The castle was besieged and rebuilt multiple times, and the current ferro-concrete reconstruction (1931) is one of Japan's most visited historic attractions. The surrounding park contains 600 cherry trees.

name: Nijō Castle
shortDescription: The Tokugawa shogunate's Kyoto fortress, famous for its 'nightingale floors'.
description: Built by Tokugawa Ieyasu as his base in Kyoto, Nijō Castle was a statement of shogunal supremacy over the imperial capital. Its Ninomaru Palace is famous for its 'nightingale floors' (uguisubari) — boards engineered to creak loudly when walked on to prevent silent approach by assassins. The gilded interiors of the Ninomaru Palace represent the peak of Japanese decorative arts of the early Edo period.

name: Kumamoto Castle
shortDescription: The 'Crow Castle of Kyushu' — famous for its distinctive black walls and curved stone bases.
description: Kumamoto Castle was considered one of Japan's three premier castles and served as a major stronghold during the 1877 Satsuma Rebellion — the last samurai uprising. Saigo Takamori's army besieged it for 54 days but failed to take it. The castle's famous stone walls, built with a gentle inward curve (nori-ji design), prevented climbing. Badly damaged by the 2016 Kumamoto earthquake, extensive restoration is ongoing.

name: Matsue Castle
shortDescription: One of Japan's twelve original castles, the only surviving tenshu in the Chugoku region.
description: Matsue Castle, known as Plover Castle (Chidori-jō), is one of only twelve original (unreconstructed) castle keeps in Japan. Completed in 1611 by Horio Yoshiharu, its five-story, six-floor black wooden exterior has survived intact. Its robust construction includes a rare double-walled main tower and is notable for the hanging openings where boiling water or stones were dropped on attackers.

name: Hirosaki Castle
shortDescription: A small but beautiful castle surrounded by Japan's most celebrated cherry blossom.
description: Hirosaki Castle in Aomori Prefecture is famous both as one of Japan's twelve original castle keeps and for the stunning cherry blossom displays surrounding it each spring — over 2,600 trees create a pink canopy over the moats. The three-story keep was built in 1611 after the original five-story tower was struck by lightning. Its compact, refined design represents Tsugaru clan aesthetics of the early Edo period.

name: Okayama Castle
shortDescription: The 'Crow Castle' of western Japan — a striking all-black fortress beside Korakuen garden.
description: Nicknamed Ujo ('Crow Castle') for its black wooden exterior, Okayama Castle was built by Ukita Hideie in 1597 and features a distinctive three-stage tower. Destroyed in WWII bombing and rebuilt in 1966, it stands alongside Korakuen — one of Japan's three greatest landscape gardens. The combination of black castle and white garden walls against the Asahi River creates one of Japan's most iconic urban views.
